questions.question: A gaming company wants to serve its global player base with the LOWEST possible latency for real-time game state updates. Players are concentrated in specific metro areas like Los Angeles, New York, and London. The company does NOT want to deploy full AWS regions in each city. Which AWS infrastructure feature BEST meets this requirement?
options: AWS Local Zones, which extend AWS compute and storage to specific metro areas for single-digit millisecond latency
AWS Edge Locations, which run EC2 instances and databases close to players in metro areas
AWS Wavelength Zones, which embed AWS compute inside 5G carrier networks for ultra-low latency mobile gaming
AWS Outposts, which deploys AWS hardware inside each player's city datacenter
questions.explanation: Local Zones are AWS infrastructure deployments in specific metro areas that bring compute, storage, and database services within single-digit milliseconds of end users. They are ideal for latency-sensitive workloads like gaming without the cost of a full region. Edge Locations are CloudFront CDN nodes that cache content — they do not run EC2 or databases. Wavelength Zones embed compute inside 5G carrier networks for ultra-low latency to 5G mobile devices, not general metro deployments. Outposts are AWS racks installed inside a customer's own on-premises datacenter, not a general city infrastructure.

questions.question: A company is designing a multi-region active-active architecture. They need to understand AWS's minimum Availability Zone guarantee per region before selecting deployment regions. Which statement CORRECTLY describes AWS AZ guarantees?
options: Each AWS region has a minimum of 2 Availability Zones, with most regions having 3 or more
Each AWS region has exactly 3 Availability Zones — no more, no less
Each AWS region has a minimum of 4 Availability Zones to ensure high availability
Availability Zones are shared across neighboring regions to maximize redundancy
questions.explanation: AWS guarantees a minimum of 2 AZs per region, though most commercial regions have 3 or more (up to 6). This minimum matters when designing for HA — selecting a region with only 2 AZs limits redundancy options. AZs are not standardized at exactly 3; regions vary. The 4-AZ minimum is incorrect. AZs are never shared across regions — each region has its own physically separate, independent AZs.

questions.question: A company has 20 VPCs across multiple AWS accounts and needs all of them to communicate with each other and share a single on-premises Direct Connect connection. The network team wants to minimize the number of connections to manage. Which service provides the MOST scalable and operationally simple solution?
options: AWS Transit Gateway, acting as a central hub that connects all 20 VPCs and the Direct Connect gateway in a hub-and-spoke topology
VPC Peering between all 20 VPCs and a dedicated VPC that terminates the Direct Connect connection
AWS VPN connections from each VPC to the on-premises network, sharing routing through the Direct Connect link
AWS PrivateLink endpoints in each VPC connecting to a shared Direct Connect virtual interface
questions.explanation: Transit Gateway is purpose-built for large-scale hub-and-spoke networking. It connects up to thousands of VPCs and on-premises networks through a single gateway, requiring only one Direct Connect Gateway attachment. VPC Peering does not support transitive routing — 20 VPCs would require up to 190 peering connections (N*(N-1)/2), creating massive operational complexity. Individual VPN connections per VPC would bypass Direct Connect and add latency and cost. PrivateLink is for exposing specific services, not transitive network routing.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate its on-premises workloads to AWS while maintaining private network connectivity (not over the public internet) AND ensuring DNS queries for internal hostnames resolve correctly across both on-premises and AWS VPCs. Which TWO components should be configured? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Direct Connect to establish a private dedicated network link between on-premises and the AWS VPC
Amazon CloudFront to provide private network connectivity between on-premises and AWS
Amazon Route 53 Resolver with inbound and outbound endpoints to forward DNS queries between on-premises DNS servers and AWS VPC DNS
AWS WAF to filter DNS queries between on-premises and AWS environments
Internet Gateway attached to the VPC to enable private connectivity with on-premises networks
questions.explanation: Direct Connect provides a private, dedicated network connection that does not traverse the public internet — required for private connectivity. Route 53 Resolver with inbound endpoints allows on-premises DNS servers to forward queries to AWS, and outbound endpoints allow VPC resources to query on-premises DNS — enabling bidirectional private DNS resolution. CloudFront is a CDN for public content, not private connectivity. WAF filters HTTP traffic, not DNS. An Internet Gateway connects a VPC to the public internet and provides no private on-premises connectivity.

questions.question: A startup runs a stateless API workload with highly variable traffic — near-zero requests overnight and massive spikes during business hours. The workload can tolerate interruptions with automatic restart. The team wants to MINIMIZE compute costs. Which EC2 pricing model is MOST cost-effective?
options: Spot Instances, which offer up to 90% discount vs On-Demand and automatically restart after interruption — suitable for stateless, fault-tolerant workloads
Reserved Instances (1-year, All Upfront), which provide predictable billing at a significant discount for steady-state usage
On-Demand Instances, which charge per hour with no commitment and scale instantly with the workload
Dedicated Hosts, which provide the lowest per-instance cost for variable workloads with compliance requirements
questions.explanation: Spot Instances are unused EC2 capacity offered at up to 90% discount. Since the API is stateless and can tolerate interruptions (and restart automatically), Spot is the ideal fit — the major risk of interruption is acceptable. Reserved Instances require a 1-3 year commitment and are only cost-effective for steady, predictable usage — not variable overnight-to-peak workloads. On-Demand has no discounts and is the most expensive option for variable workloads. Dedicated Hosts are the most expensive option, designed for compliance needs, not cost minimization.

questions.question: A company needs to run nightly batch jobs that process large datasets. The jobs run for 2-6 hours each night, require 500 GB of memory, and must complete by 6 AM. Cost optimization is the primary goal, and the jobs can be automatically retried if interrupted. Which AWS compute service BEST fits this use case?
options: AWS Batch with Spot EC2 instances, which automatically provisions and terminates compute for batch jobs and retries interrupted tasks
Amazon EC2 On-Demand instances in an Auto Scaling group scaled out before the batch window and terminated after
AWS Lambda functions triggered on a schedule to run the batch processing jobs
Amazon ECS with Fargate to run the batch jobs as containers on a nightly schedule
questions.explanation: AWS Batch is designed specifically for batch workloads — it automatically provisions EC2 (or Spot) instances, manages job queues, handles retries on interruption, and terminates resources when jobs complete. Using Spot instances with AWS Batch maximizes cost savings while the automatic retry feature handles Spot interruptions. Manual EC2 Auto Scaling requires more operational overhead. Lambda has a 15-minute maximum execution time and 10 GB memory limit — insufficient for 2-6 hour, 500 GB memory jobs. ECS Fargate is a containerized runtime that is less integrated with batch job scheduling and Spot retry logic than AWS Batch.

questions.question: A company stores 500 TB of compliance documents in Amazon S3. Documents are accessed frequently for the first 30 days after creation, then rarely accessed — but must be retrievable within 1 millisecond when needed (even after 30 days). The company wants to MINIMIZE storage costs. Which S3 storage class configuration BEST meets this requirement?
options: S3 Intelligent-Tiering, which automatically moves objects between frequent-access and infrequent-access tiers and offers an Archive Instant Access tier for millisecond retrieval of rarely-accessed data
S3 Standard for 30 days followed by a lifecycle policy to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval for long-term storage
S3 Standard-IA for all objects, providing low-cost storage with millisecond retrieval for infrequently accessed data
S3 Standard for 30 days followed by a lifecycle policy to S3 One Zone-IA for cost-effective infrequent access
questions.explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage patterns with no retrieval fees. Its Archive Instant Access tier provides millisecond retrieval (unlike Glacier Flexible Retrieval which takes minutes to hours), making it ideal when retrieval time is critical but access is unpredictable. Glacier Flexible Retrieval takes minutes to hours — it violates the 1ms retrieval requirement. S3 Standard-IA charges per retrieval and is better for known infrequent access patterns, but Intelligent-Tiering automates the optimization without per-retrieval fees. One Zone-IA stores data in only one AZ — acceptable for replaceable data but not compliance documents requiring durability.

questions.question: A financial services company runs a database on an EC2 instance that requires extremely high I/O performance — 64,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s throughput. The data is critical and must persist if the EC2 instance is stopped or terminated. Which storage option BEST meets these requirements?
options: Amazon EBS io2 Block Express volume, which supports up to 256,000 IOPS and 4,000 MB/s throughput for the highest performance persistent block storage
EC2 Instance Store (NVMe), which provides the highest possible IOPS but data is lost when the instance stops or terminates
Amazon EFS with Provisioned Throughput, which provides persistent shared file storage with configurable throughput
Amazon S3 with S3 Transfer Acceleration for high-throughput database I/O with full data persistence
questions.explanation: EBS io2 Block Express supports up to 256,000 IOPS and 4,000 MB/s throughput — well above the requirements — and data persists independently of the EC2 instance lifecycle. Instance Store offers the highest raw IOPS but is ephemeral: data is permanently lost when the instance stops or terminates, violating the persistence requirement. EFS is a shared NFS file system with lower per-instance IOPS than EBS io2 and is not designed for database block I/O. S3 is object storage accessed via HTTP API — it cannot serve as a block device for a database engine.

questions.question: A social network application needs a database that can handle millions of user profile lookups per second with microsecond latency. The data model is simple key-value pairs. The workload is unpredictable and must scale automatically without capacity planning. Which database solution is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon DynamoDB with DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX), providing serverless NoSQL with microsecond read latency at any scale
Amazon Aurora MySQL with Read Replicas to distribute the millions of read requests across multiple nodes
Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as the primary database, storing all user profiles in-memory for microsecond access
Amazon RDS PostgreSQL with connection pooling via RDS Proxy to handle millions of concurrent connections
questions.explanation: DynamoDB is a serverless NoSQL database that scales automatically to handle millions of requests per second. DAX is an in-memory cache layer in front of DynamoDB that reduces read latency from single-digit milliseconds to microseconds. Aurora Read Replicas handle high read throughput but have millisecond (not microsecond) latency and require capacity planning. ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory cache, not a durable primary database — data loss on node failure is a concern. RDS PostgreSQL with connection pooling does not scale to millions of concurrent requests at microsecond latency without significant sharding complexity.

questions.question: A startup is building a new application and must choose between Amazon Aurora and standard Amazon RDS MySQL for their relational database. Which TWO characteristics correctly describe Aurora advantages over standard RDS MySQL? (Choose TWO)
options: Aurora automatically replicates data 6 times across 3 Availability Zones, providing higher durability than standard RDS Multi-AZ which maintains 2 copies
Aurora supports automatic failover in under 30 seconds, while standard RDS Multi-AZ failover typically takes 1-2 minutes
Aurora stores data in the same single AZ as the primary instance, requiring manual cross-AZ replication setup
Aurora charges are identical to standard RDS MySQL with no premium for the Aurora storage layer
Aurora can only be used with MySQL and does not support PostgreSQL-compatible engines
questions.explanation: Aurora maintains 6 copies of data across 3 AZs by default (compared to RDS Multi-AZ which keeps 2 copies — primary and standby), making it significantly more durable. Aurora's storage is distributed across AZs by design and automatic failover completes in under 30 seconds (compared to the typical 1-2 minute RDS Multi-AZ failover window). Aurora does NOT store data in a single AZ — its distributed storage across 3 AZs is a core feature. Aurora does carry a premium over standard RDS MySQL pricing. Aurora supports both MySQL-compatible and PostgreSQL-compatible engines.

questions.question: An order management system publishes order-placed events. Three downstream services must each receive every event: inventory, notifications, and analytics. Each service processes events at its own rate and must not be blocked by slow processing in another service. Which messaging pattern is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon SNS topic with three SQS Standard queue subscriptions — one per service — for fan-out with independent consumption
A single SQS Standard queue where all three services compete to consume each message
Amazon SQS FIFO queue with three consumer groups each receiving all messages
Amazon EventBridge with three separate buses routing the order event to each downstream service
questions.explanation: The SNS fan-out to SQS pattern ensures every downstream service receives every message independently. SNS delivers the message to all subscribed SQS queues simultaneously; each service reads from its own queue at its own pace without affecting others. A single SQS Standard queue uses competing-consumer model — each message is delivered to only ONE consumer, so two services would miss messages. SQS FIFO does not support multiple independent consumer groups where each receives all messages. EventBridge on separate buses is a valid pattern but less common than SNS-SQS fan-out for decoupled multi-consumer scenarios.

questions.question: A company migrates from an on-premises message broker running RabbitMQ with AMQP protocol. Their existing applications cannot be modified to use a different messaging protocol. Which AWS messaging service should they use to MINIMIZE migration effort?
options: Amazon MQ, which provides managed ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ brokers supporting AMQP, MQTT, and other standard messaging protocols
Amazon SQS, which supports AMQP protocol natively and provides a managed message queue
Amazon SNS, which supports AMQP for push-based message delivery to existing RabbitMQ clients
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, which supports AMQP protocol for real-time streaming with RabbitMQ clients
questions.explanation: Amazon MQ is the managed message broker service that supports RabbitMQ natively with AMQP — applications can connect using their existing RabbitMQ client libraries without code changes. SQS uses AWS-specific APIs (not AMQP) and would require application code changes. SNS does not support AMQP; it uses AWS SDKs or HTTP endpoints. Kinesis uses AWS-specific producer/consumer APIs, not AMQP protocol.

questions.question: A DevOps team wants to implement a full CI/CD pipeline on AWS: automatically build and test code on every commit, deploy to staging with approval gates, and finally deploy to production. They want to use only AWS-native services with LEAST operational overhead. Which combination BEST achieves this?
options: AWS CodePipeline to orchestrate the pipeline stages; AWS CodeBuild for building and testing; AWS CodeDeploy for deploying to staging and production with manual approval actions
AWS CodeCommit to run the pipeline; AWS CodeBuild for deployment; AWS Lambda for approval gates
AWS CodeDeploy to orchestrate the full pipeline; AWS CodeBuild for testing; Amazon SNS for deployment notifications
AWS CodeStar to manage the pipeline; AWS Elastic Beanstalk for build/test; AWS CodeCommit for deployments
questions.explanation: CodePipeline is the CI/CD orchestration service that chains pipeline stages (Source, Build, Test, Deploy) with built-in manual approval actions between stages. CodeBuild handles compilation and test execution in isolated managed build environments. CodeDeploy automates deployments to EC2, Lambda, or ECS. Together they form a fully managed AWS-native CI/CD pipeline. CodeCommit is a Git repository, not a pipeline runner. CodeDeploy alone cannot orchestrate multi-stage pipelines. CodeStar is a project management dashboard that wraps these same services but adds unnecessary abstraction.

questions.question: A development team reports that their microservices application has intermittent latency spikes. The application spans 8 Lambda functions and 3 DynamoDB tables. The team needs to identify WHICH service call in the chain is causing the delay. Which AWS service provides the BEST visibility into end-to-end request flow and latency breakdown?
options: AWS X-Ray, which traces requests across Lambda functions and DynamoDB calls, producing a service map with per-segment latency breakdown
Amazon CloudWatch Metrics, which collects per-service average latency metrics for Lambda and DynamoDB
AWS CloudTrail, which logs every API call timestamp enabling manual calculation of inter-service latency
Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights, which queries Lambda log streams to reconstruct the request flow
questions.explanation: X-Ray provides distributed tracing — it instruments Lambda functions and AWS SDK calls to DynamoDB, producing an end-to-end service map with subsegment-level latency for each hop in the call chain. This directly identifies which service call is the bottleneck. CloudWatch Metrics shows aggregate statistics per service but cannot correlate individual request traces across multiple services. CloudTrail records API events for governance/auditing, not distributed tracing with latency attribution. CloudWatch Logs Insights can query individual Lambda log streams but cannot automatically correlate requests across multiple services into a trace.

questions.question: A company wants to automatically receive recommendations on how to rightsize their underutilized EC2 instances and Lambda functions to reduce costs — without performing manual analysis. Which AWS service provides these automated rightsizing recommendations?
options: AWS Compute Optimizer, which analyzes CloudWatch utilization metrics and uses ML to recommend optimal EC2 instance types and Lambda memory configurations
AWS Trusted Advisor, which provides EC2 rightsizing recommendations across all AWS accounts at no cost
AWS Cost Explorer, which provides per-resource rightsizing recommendations based on billing data
Amazon CloudWatch, which automatically identifies underutilized instances and suggests optimal instance types
questions.explanation: Compute Optimizer analyzes 14 days of CloudWatch metrics using machine learning and recommends optimal EC2 instance types, Lambda memory settings, EBS volume configurations, and ECS task sizes. It is purpose-built for automated rightsizing. Trusted Advisor provides some rightsizing checks but requires Business or Enterprise Support for full recommendations and is less granular than Compute Optimizer. Cost Explorer shows historical spend and has some RI recommendations but does not provide ML-based rightsizing. CloudWatch collects metrics but does not automatically recommend instance types.

questions.question: A security auditor needs to prove that no unauthorized AWS API calls were made in any of the company's 15 accounts over the past year. The logs must be stored immutably in a single S3 bucket in a dedicated logging account. Which AWS service and configuration satisfies this requirement?
options: AWS CloudTrail with an Organization Trail enabled, delivering logs from all 15 accounts to a centralized S3 bucket in the logging account with S3 Object Lock for immutability
Amazon CloudWatch Logs aggregated from all 15 accounts into a central log group in the logging account
AWS Config with an aggregator collecting resource configuration history across all 15 accounts into the logging account
AWS Security Hub with cross-account findings aggregation forwarded to an S3 bucket in the logging account
questions.explanation: CloudTrail Organization Trail automatically captures API activity from all accounts in an AWS Organization and delivers consolidated logs to a designated S3 bucket. Enabling S3 Object Lock (WORM) on that bucket prevents log tampering, satisfying the immutability requirement. CloudWatch Logs captures application-level logs, not AWS API call history. Config tracks resource configuration changes but its aggregator does not capture full API call logs. Security Hub aggregates security findings, not raw API call logs.

questions.question: A company builds a mobile app backend using AWS Lambda. When a user uploads a profile photo to S3, a Lambda function must automatically resize it to three different resolutions. The team wants ZERO server management and the solution must scale automatically with the number of uploads. Which architecture is MOST appropriate?
options: Configure an S3 Event Notification to trigger a Lambda function on s3:ObjectCreated events; the Lambda function performs the three resizing operations
Configure a CloudWatch Events rule on a fixed schedule to scan S3 for new uploads and trigger Lambda for resizing
Use Amazon SQS to queue upload events and poll from an EC2 Auto Scaling group running the resizing code
Deploy an ECS Fargate task that polls S3 for new objects every minute and performs the resizing
questions.explanation: S3 Event Notifications trigger Lambda immediately and automatically when objects are created — no polling, no servers. Lambda scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions automatically, and charges only per invocation. A CloudWatch scheduled rule introduces latency (minutes between scans) and is not event-driven. SQS with EC2 requires managing Auto Scaling groups, capacity, and patching — high operational overhead. ECS Fargate with polling adds latency and wastes compute between uploads; it also requires managing task scheduling.

questions.question: A startup wants to build a fully serverless REST API that authenticates users with username/password and throttles requests per user to 100 requests per minute. Which TWO AWS services should be combined to achieve this with LEAST operational overhead? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon API Gateway to create, publish, and throttle REST API endpoints with per-client usage plans
Amazon EC2 with Nginx to host the REST API and implement per-user rate limiting
Amazon Cognito User Pools as an authorizer for API Gateway, providing user authentication and JWT token validation
AWS IAM users to authenticate each API caller and enforce per-user throttling limits
Amazon CloudFront as the API endpoint with Lambda@Edge for authentication and throttling
questions.explanation: API Gateway provides managed REST API hosting with built-in usage plans and throttling per API key or per user — no infrastructure to manage. Cognito User Pools provides user sign-up, sign-in, and JWT tokens that API Gateway can validate natively as a Cognito authorizer, handling authentication without custom code. EC2 with Nginx requires infrastructure management. IAM users are for AWS service access, not application-level user authentication and throttling. CloudFront with Lambda@Edge adds complexity and is not the standard pattern for serverless REST APIs.

questions.question: A company's AWS bill has been unexpectedly high. The CFO wants to: (1) understand which teams and projects are driving costs, and (2) receive an automatic alert if monthly spend exceeds $50,000. Which combination of AWS Cost Management features addresses BOTH needs?
options: Cost Allocation Tags to attribute costs to teams/projects; AWS Budgets to send an alert when monthly spend exceeds $50,000
AWS Cost Explorer to automatically tag resources by team; AWS Billing Alerts via CloudWatch to alert on spend
AWS Trusted Advisor to identify cost drivers by team; AWS Budgets to alert when spend exceeds the threshold
AWS Organizations consolidated billing to attribute costs by account; AWS Cost Explorer to alert when spend exceeds $50,000
questions.explanation: Cost Allocation Tags (e.g., Team=engineering, Project=alpha) are applied to resources and appear in billing reports, enabling cost attribution by team or project in Cost Explorer. AWS Budgets can set a fixed dollar threshold and send email/SNS alerts when actual or forecasted spend crosses it. Cost Explorer visualizes historical data but does not auto-tag resources or send proactive alerts. Trusted Advisor identifies cost optimization opportunities (idle instances, etc.) but does not attribute costs by team. Consolidated billing rolls up accounts but does not tag resources by team within an account.

questions.question: A company runs EC2 instances 24/7 for a stable production workload that has been running for 2 years and is expected to continue for at least 2 more years. The team wants to MAXIMIZE cost savings while maintaining the flexibility to change instance families if needed in the future. Which purchasing option BEST meets this?
options: Compute Savings Plans (3-year commitment), which offer up to 66% savings and apply automatically across any EC2 instance family, size, region, or OS
Standard Reserved Instances (3-year, All Upfront) for the specific instance type, providing up to 72% savings with no flexibility to change
Convertible Reserved Instances (3-year), which offer savings and allow conversion to a different instance family at equal or greater value
On-Demand Instances with aggressive rightsizing, avoiding any long-term commitment
questions.explanation: Compute Savings Plans provide up to 66% savings over On-Demand and automatically apply to any EC2 instance regardless of family, size, AZ, region, or OS — giving maximum flexibility. The 3-year term maximizes savings. Standard Reserved Instances offer up to 72% savings but lock in a specific instance type — changing families requires selling on the RI Marketplace, adding friction. Convertible RIs allow family changes but offer less savings (up to 66%) than Standard RIs, and are less flexible than Savings Plans which apply automatically. On-Demand with rightsizing misses significant savings for a 2+ year stable workload.

questions.question: A company stores highly sensitive encryption keys that are used by a custom application running on EC2. A compliance requirement mandates that the company must have sole control over the HSM hardware and the keys must never leave the dedicated hardware module. Which AWS service meets this requirement?
options: AWS CloudHSM, which provides a dedicated, single-tenant HSM appliance deployed in the customer's VPC where only the customer controls the keys
AWS KMS with Customer Managed Keys (CMK), which gives the customer full control over encryption keys in a managed multi-tenant service
AWS Secrets Manager, which stores encryption keys with customer-controlled access policies and automatic rotation
AWS Certificate Manager (ACM), which manages private encryption keys for SSL/TLS with customer-controlled access
questions.explanation: CloudHSM provides single-tenant HSM appliances where the customer holds the hardware security module exclusively and AWS has no access to the keys. This satisfies compliance requirements that mandate dedicated hardware and sole customer control. KMS is a multi-tenant service where AWS manages the underlying HSM infrastructure — AWS technically has access to key material at the service level. Secrets Manager stores application secrets (passwords, API keys), not cryptographic key material for custom applications. ACM manages TLS certificates and their private keys for AWS services but does not provide a customer-controlled HSM.

questions.question: A company suspects their S3 buckets contain undetected sensitive customer data (PII, credit card numbers). They want an automated service that uses machine learning to continuously scan and classify the sensitive data in S3. Which AWS service is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon Macie, which uses ML to automatically discover, classify, and alert on sensitive data (PII, financial data) in Amazon S3
Amazon GuardDuty, which uses ML to scan S3 objects for sensitive data and generates compliance findings
AWS Inspector, which scans S3 buckets for sensitive data patterns including PII and financial information
Amazon Comprehend, which continuously monitors S3 buckets for PII and sends alerts when sensitive data is found
questions.explanation: Macie is specifically designed to discover and classify sensitive data in S3 using ML — it identifies PII (names, addresses, SSNs), financial data (credit card numbers), and other sensitive content, then generates findings and alerts. GuardDuty detects threats and unusual behavior in CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS — it does not scan S3 object content for PII. Inspector assesses software vulnerabilities and security configurations on EC2 and container images, not S3 data content. Comprehend is an NLP service that processes text for entity extraction — it does not continuously monitor or scan S3 buckets.

questions.question: A company runs hundreds of EC2 instances. Security engineers need to access instances for troubleshooting without opening inbound port 22 (SSH), without managing SSH key pairs, and with a full audit log of every session. Which AWS service enables this?
options: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, which provides browser-based or CLI shell access to EC2 instances without SSH ports, key pairs, or bastion hosts — all sessions are logged
AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager, which provides secure shell access to EC2 instances for patching without SSH
Amazon EC2 Instance Connect, which provides temporary SSH access without managing long-term key pairs but still requires port 22 open
AWS Direct Connect, which provides a private network path to EC2 instances that eliminates the need for SSH key management
questions.explanation: SSM Session Manager provides encrypted, interactive shell access to EC2 instances (with the SSM Agent installed) through the SSM API — no inbound ports, no SSH keys, no bastion hosts required. Every session is logged to CloudWatch Logs or S3 for full auditability. Patch Manager automates patching and does not provide interactive shell access. EC2 Instance Connect provides temporary SSH key injection but still requires port 22 open inbound. Direct Connect is a private network link between on-premises and AWS — it does not eliminate SSH requirements.

questions.question: A large enterprise wants to ensure all new AWS accounts created within their AWS Organization automatically have a pre-approved set of VPCs, IAM roles, and security baselines configured — with preventive controls that automatically stop account owners from disabling CloudTrail. Which service is MOST purpose-built for automated multi-account governance?
options: AWS Control Tower, which automates secure multi-account setup via Account Factory and enforces preventive and detective guardrails across the Organization
AWS CloudFormation StackSets, which deploys baseline infrastructure stacks to new accounts but requires manual trigger for each new account
AWS Organizations with Service Control Policies, which enforces preventive controls but does not automatically provision account baselines
AWS Config with Conformance Packs, which detects and remediates configuration drift but does not automate initial account setup
questions.explanation: Control Tower is the purpose-built service for automated multi-account governance. Account Factory automatically provisions new accounts with a configurable baseline (VPCs, IAM roles, logging). Guardrails — implemented as SCPs and Config rules — enforce preventive controls (e.g., deny cloudtrail:StopLogging) automatically across all enrolled accounts. CloudFormation StackSets can deploy stacks to multiple accounts but require manual triggers and do not provide Account Factory automation or integrated guardrail management. SCPs alone enforce restrictions but do not auto-provision baselines. Config Conformance Packs detect drift after the fact but do not prevent violations or automate initial setup.

questions.question: A company uses AWS Organizations with 25 accounts. Employees use Microsoft Azure AD as their corporate identity provider. The CISO wants employees to log into any AWS account using their Azure AD credentials without creating IAM users. They also want access to be managed from a central location. Which AWS service achieves this with LEAST overhead?
options: AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) configured with Azure AD as an external SAML 2.0 identity provider, enabling SSO across all 25 AWS accounts
AWS Directory Service Managed Microsoft AD synced with Azure AD, assigning IAM roles to synced users per account
Amazon Cognito Identity Pools federated with Azure AD, exchanging Azure tokens for temporary AWS credentials in each account
AWS IAM with SAML 2.0 federation configured separately in each of the 25 accounts to trust Azure AD tokens
questions.explanation: IAM Identity Center supports external IdPs via SAML 2.0 — Azure AD can be connected as the identity source. Once configured, employees sign in once with Azure AD credentials and access any of the 25 accounts from a single SSO portal with permission sets managed centrally. This is the purpose-built AWS SSO solution. AWS Directory Service syncing with Azure AD adds significant complexity and is typically used for domain-joined workloads, not console SSO. Cognito Identity Pools are for application-level AWS credential vending, not enterprise console SSO. Per-account SAML federation requires configuring trust in each of the 25 accounts separately — massive operational overhead.

questions.question: A insurance company receives thousands of scanned paper claim forms as PDF images daily. They want to automatically extract the structured data (form fields, tables, and checkboxes) from these scanned documents and store the results in CSV format for downstream processing. Which AWS ML service is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon Textract, which performs OCR on scanned documents and extracts text, form fields, tables, and key-value pairs — outputting structured data suitable for CSV
Amazon Comprehend, which analyzes text from scanned PDFs to extract structured entities like names, dates, and amounts
Amazon Rekognition, which reads text from scanned document images using its text detection API
Amazon Kendra, which indexes scanned claim forms and allows structured queries to extract field values
questions.explanation: Textract is purpose-built for document extraction from scanned images and PDFs — it goes beyond OCR to understand document structure: form key-value pairs, tables, and checkboxes. It outputs structured data that can be serialized to CSV. Comprehend is an NLP service that analyzes raw text for sentiment, entities, and PII — it cannot process scanned image files directly and does not extract table structure. Rekognition's text detection reads text in images but does not understand document structure (forms, tables). Kendra is an enterprise search service for querying document content — not for structured extraction from scanned forms.

questions.question: An e-commerce company wants to add a customer service chatbot that can understand natural language, conduct multi-turn conversations, and integrate with their order management backend via Lambda. Which AWS service is MOST appropriate for building this chatbot?
options: Amazon Lex, which provides automatic speech recognition and natural language understanding to build conversational chatbots that integrate with Lambda for backend fulfillment
Amazon Polly, which converts text responses to lifelike speech for customer interactions in the chatbot
Amazon Transcribe, which enables voice-based chatbot interactions by converting customer speech to text
Amazon Comprehend, which understands customer intent from text messages and routes to the appropriate response
questions.explanation: Amazon Lex is the service for building conversational AI chatbots — it provides NLU (natural language understanding) to identify intents and slots from customer messages, manages multi-turn dialog, and natively integrates with Lambda functions for backend fulfillment (looking up orders, processing returns). Polly converts text to speech — it is used for voice output, not understanding input. Transcribe converts speech to text — useful as an input component but does not provide conversation management or intent recognition. Comprehend extracts sentiment and entities from text but does not manage conversational dialog flows.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has 800 TB of production data on an on-premises SQL Server database and 200 TB of archived files on NAS storage. They want to migrate both to AWS within 6 months. The SQL Server migration must keep the source database running during migration (minimal downtime). The NAS files should be migrated online over an existing 10 Gbps Direct Connect link. Which TWO services should be used? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) to continuously replicate the SQL Server database to Amazon RDS or Aurora with the source remaining live
AWS Snowball to physically transport the SQL Server database data to AWS for the one-time migration
AWS DataSync over the Direct Connect link to migrate the 200 TB of NAS files online to Amazon S3 or EFS
AWS Storage Gateway to migrate the 200 TB of NAS files to Amazon S3 over the Direct Connect link
AWS Transfer Family to migrate the NAS files to S3 over the Direct Connect link using SFTP
questions.explanation: DMS is designed for live database migrations — the source database stays fully operational while DMS replicates changes continuously using CDC (Change Data Capture). For heterogeneous migrations like SQL Server to Aurora, DMS + Schema Conversion Tool handles schema differences. DataSync is the purpose-built service for online file migration over Direct Connect — it efficiently transfers large datasets over dedicated connections to S3, EFS, or FSx with automatic integrity verification. Snowball is for offline bulk transfers when the network is insufficient — here a 10 Gbps Direct Connect is available. Storage Gateway provides ongoing hybrid access, not one-time bulk migration. Transfer Family provides SFTP endpoints for ongoing file uploads, not a batch migration tool.

questions.question: A company needs to assess the business case and estimated AWS costs for migrating 500 on-premises servers to AWS before getting budget approval. They have not yet begun any migration activities. Which AWS service is MOST appropriate for this initial planning phase?
options: AWS Migration Evaluator, which analyzes on-premises inventory and resource utilization data to produce a business case with estimated AWS costs
AWS Application Discovery Service, which deploys agents to collect server utilization data for migration planning
AWS Migration Hub, which tracks the progress of servers being migrated and provides cost estimates
AWS Database Migration Service, which assesses on-premises database schemas and produces migration cost reports
questions.explanation: Migration Evaluator (formerly TSO Logic) is the purpose-built tool for the pre-migration business case phase. It analyzes on-premises resource utilization data and produces rightsized AWS recommendations with cost projections — the output is a business case document for budget approval. Application Discovery Service collects utilization data for migration planning but produces raw inventory, not a cost business case. Migration Hub tracks migration execution progress — it is used during migration, not before it. DMS migrates databases actively; it does not produce business case cost assessments.

questions.question: A fintech company streams 500,000 financial transactions per second from global exchanges. They need to run real-time SQL analytics on this stream — detecting anomalies like sudden price spikes within a 30-second sliding window — and deliver results to Amazon Redshift for further analysis. Which combination of Kinesis services BEST meets this?
options: Kinesis Data Streams to ingest the transaction stream; Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) to run real-time SQL with sliding window queries; Kinesis Firehose to deliver aggregated results to Redshift
Kinesis Firehose to ingest the transaction stream; Kinesis Data Analytics for real-time SQL queries; Kinesis Data Streams to deliver results to Redshift
Kinesis Data Streams to ingest transactions; Amazon Athena for real-time sliding window SQL queries on the stream; Firehose to deliver to Redshift
Kinesis Firehose to ingest the transaction stream directly into Redshift; Redshift Spectrum for real-time streaming SQL analysis
questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Streams handles high-throughput real-time ingestion with millisecond latency — appropriate for 500,000 TPS. Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) provides serverless stream processing with SQL support for windowed aggregations (sliding windows) on the live stream. Kinesis Firehose delivers the processed results to Redshift with automatic batching and format conversion. Firehose cannot be the ingestion layer for Kinesis Data Analytics — Data Analytics reads from Data Streams or Firehose, not the other way around. Athena is a batch SQL engine for S3 data, not a real-time stream processor. Redshift Spectrum queries historical data in S3 — it does not process live streams.
